scenario: Trivy scan of production image python:3.9-slim returned: CVE-2024-3094 (xz-utils backdoor, CVSS 10.0, fixed in 5.6.1+). The image is used by 3 production deployments serving customer traffic on internet-facing services. Patch advisory has been available since 2024-04-01. Produce the BERU finding for this scanner output.
expected_actions: Cite SI-2 Flaw Remediation as primary control
Cite RA-5 Vulnerability Monitoring as supporting
Mark STATUS as FAIL
Assign C-rank with explicit Likelihood Impact justification
Draft POA&M with scheduled completion within 30 days
Identify EVIDENCE GAP: patch deployment timeline not documented
expected_evidence_gap: patch deployment timeline
rollback procedure
patched vs unpatched count across the 3 deployments

scenario: kube-bench check 5.2.1 returned FAIL: 'Minimize the admission of privileged containers'. Inspection shows 4 pods in the default namespace running with securityContext.privileged=true. Three are application workloads; one is a legitimate node-monitoring agent. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite CM-7 Least Functionality as primary control
Cite AC-6 Least Privilege as supporting
Mark STATUS FAIL because 3 of 4 are application workloads with no documented justification
C-rank because privileged container is a known container-escape vector
POA&M to remove privileged flag from 3 application workloads
Note EVIDENCE GAP: no PSP or admission policy preventing future regressions
expected_evidence_gap: admission control policy preventing future privileged containers
owner of node-monitoring agent justification document
Pod Security Standards baseline enforcement state

scenario: Prowler check s3_bucket_default_encryption returned FAIL on bucket prod-customer-records. The bucket holds PII covered by HIPAA. Bucket policy shows public-read denied and TLS-only enforced, but server-side encryption is not configured. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite SC-28 Protection of Information at Rest as primary
Cite SC-13 Cryptographic Protection as supporting
Mark STATUS FAIL
Assign B-rank because of HIPAA scope and PII exposure
Escalate per BERU authority ceiling — B-rank requires human approval
POA&M for SSE-KMS enablement with key rotation
expected_evidence_gap: KMS key rotation policy
audit log of past unencrypted writes
data residency / region compliance

scenario: Semgrep ruleset detect-hardcoded-secret returned a finding in src/auth/legacy.py line 47: `aws_access_key = 'AKIAEXAMPLEKEY1234'`. Git blame shows the line was added 18 months ago by a developer who has since left. The repo is private but cloned by 12 engineers. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite IA-5 Authenticator Management as primary
Cite SC-28 Protection at Rest as supporting
STATUS FAIL
C-rank because key may be valid and was exposed to 12 clones for 18 months
POA&M: rotate the key immediately, audit CloudTrail for use, rewrite git history
EVIDENCE GAP: CloudTrail audit for use of this key over 18 months
expected_evidence_gap: CloudTrail audit of key usage
list of all 12 engineer clones
evidence the key has not been pushed to a public fork

scenario: Falco rule 'Terminal shell in container' fired 6 times in the last hour, all from pod api-server-0 in namespace prod. Investigation shows 5 are from a developer running `kubectl exec` for debugging; 1 has no matching audit log entry. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite SI-4 System Monitoring as primary
Cite IR-4 Incident Handling as supporting
STATUS PARTIAL because 5 of 6 are explained but 1 is not
C-rank because unexplained shell-in-container is a runtime compromise indicator
POA&M: investigate the unmatched event, restrict kubectl exec via RBAC
EVIDENCE GAP: source of the unmatched shell event
expected_evidence_gap: source IP and identity of the unmatched shell event
RBAC review of kubectl exec permissions
alert tuning to reduce false-positive rate

scenario: kube-bench check 4.2.1 ('Ensure --audit-log-path argument is set') returned PASS. The audit log file exists. No other audit-related checks were run. Produce the BERU finding — what should BERU note about evidence gaps?
expected_actions: Cite AU-2 Auditable Events as primary
Cite AU-11 Audit Record Retention as the gap
STATUS PARTIAL because PASS on 4.2.1 alone is insufficient evidence of audit compliance
Identify EVIDENCE GAP: retention period, log destination, integrity protection, content of events captured
Note that kube-bench did not test 4.2.2 through 4.2.13 — those need to run before STATUS can advance to PASS
D-rank because no immediate exposure but compliance evidence is incomplete
expected_evidence_gap: audit log retention period
audit log destination and integrity protection
list of events captured (compared to AU-2 baseline)
results of kube-bench checks 4.2.2 through 4.2.13

scenario: Prowler check s3_bucket_default_encryption returned PASS on prod-customer-records: encryption is SSE-S3 with AWS-managed keys. No other encryption-related Prowler checks were run. Produce the BERU finding identifying the evidence gaps for full SC-28 / SC-12 coverage.
expected_actions: Cite SC-28 Protection at Rest as primary
Cite SC-12 Cryptographic Key Establishment and Management as the supporting control with the largest gap
STATUS PARTIAL — SSE-S3 PASS does not establish key rotation, key access control, or BYOK posture
Identify EVIDENCE GAP: key rotation cadence, KMS-managed vs S3-managed key policy, IAM policy for KMS Decrypt
D-rank because data is encrypted but key governance is unverified
expected_evidence_gap: key rotation policy and last-rotated date
IAM policy enumerating KMS Decrypt permissions
audit log of key access events
CMK vs AWS-managed key decision documentation

scenario: Trivy image scan of prod application image returned: 0 HIGH, 0 CRITICAL, 14 LOW. Image base is python:3.11-slim pulled from Docker Hub. The team ships no SBOM with the build artifact. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite SR-3 Supply Chain Controls as primary
Cite SR-4 Provenance as the major gap
STATUS PARTIAL — Trivy clean does not establish supply chain integrity without SBOM and provenance
Identify EVIDENCE GAP: SBOM, base image signature verification, builder identity, registry access controls
C-rank because supply chain compromise is a documented enterprise threat
POA&M: generate SBOM at build time, sign images with cosign
expected_evidence_gap: SBOM at build time
image signature (cosign / Notary)
base image source attestation
registry access audit log

scenario: Semgrep auto-config returned 0 findings on the application repository. The team has no documented Semgrep ruleset under version control and no CI integration. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite SA-11 Developer Testing and Evaluation as primary
Cite CM-3 Configuration Change Control as supporting (ruleset versioning gap)
STATUS PARTIAL — clean Semgrep run with no documented ruleset is meaningless evidence
Identify EVIDENCE GAP: ruleset under version control, CI integration, exception process for findings, periodic ruleset review
D-rank because gap is procedural not active vulnerability
expected_evidence_gap: Semgrep ruleset committed to repo
CI workflow that runs Semgrep on every PR
process for handling findings
periodic review of ruleset for new rules

scenario: Falco daemonset is running in the cluster. Logs are being generated at /var/log/falco.log on each node. No retention policy is documented and logs are rotated by logrotate at 7 days. Produce the BERU finding for AU-11 / SI-4 evidence completeness.
expected_actions: Cite AU-11 Audit Record Retention as primary
Cite SI-4 System Monitoring as supporting
STATUS PARTIAL — Falco is running but 7-day local-only retention is below typical compliance requirements
Identify EVIDENCE GAP: organization audit log retention policy, log shipping to a central SIEM, retention period documentation, integrity protection
D-rank because monitoring exists but evidence chain is incomplete
POA&M: ship Falco logs to centralized SIEM, document retention to match policy
expected_evidence_gap: organization audit log retention policy document
central SIEM integration
integrity protection on Falco logs
documented retention period justification

scenario: Client AI assistant 'Acme-CompliantBot' is a customer-facing chatbot built on Azure OpenAI GPT-4. Garak prompt-injection probe returned 47 successful injections out of 100 attempts. The bot has access to a knowledge base of internal company policies. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite both NIST 800-53 SI-3 (malicious code protection) and SI-10 (information input validation)
Cite both AI RMF MEASURE 2.7 and MEASURE 2.10
Cite MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051 (LLM Prompt Injection)
STATUS FAIL
B-rank because chatbot is customer-facing and accesses internal policies — escalate to human
POA&M: input validation layer, output filter, injection-resistant prompt design
expected_evidence_gap: whether successful injections leaked confidential policy content
whether session context contaminated subsequent users
vendor escalation channel with Azure

scenario: MLflow audit of client production model 'Acme-FraudDetector-v3.7' shows: model is in production, no MLflow run is associated with the deployed weights, no params.yaml is recorded, no eval metrics are logged. The team confirms they fine-tuned the model 'using the standard recipe' but cannot reproduce the run. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite CM-2 Baseline Configuration and CM-3 Configuration Change Control
Cite AI RMF MAP 2.2 (training data and provenance documented) and MANAGE 2.4 (model lifecycle management)
Cite AML.T0048 (Backdoor ML Model) as the latent risk a backdoor cannot be ruled out without lineage
STATUS FAIL
C-rank — model is in prod making decisions with no reproducibility evidence
POA&M: re-create training pipeline under MLflow, retrain with full lineage, swap to reproducible artifact
expected_evidence_gap: original training config
training data integrity hash
evaluation metrics for the deployed weights
evidence that weights have not been tampered with since training

scenario: Vendor's AI agent 'PolicyAssist-v2' is in pilot at the client. The agent has a fixed system prompt loaded at deploy time, but there is no review process for changes to that system prompt. Vendor's documentation says 'we update the prompt as needed.' Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite CM-2 Baseline Configuration and CM-6 Configuration Settings
Cite AI RMF GOVERN 1.1 (policies and procedures for AI risk management) and MAP 2.3 (limitations documented)
STATUS PARTIAL — agent works today but configuration governance is absent
C-rank — silent prompt changes can introduce safety regressions invisible to client
POA&M: contract change requiring vendor change-control on system prompt with notification SLA
EVIDENCE GAP: vendor change log, contractual SLA on prompt changes
expected_evidence_gap: vendor's change log for the system prompt
contractual notification requirement on prompt changes
client's process for re-evaluating after a prompt change

scenario: Client's AI compliance assistant was fine-tuned on a dataset described as 'collected from public sources, anonymized, and labeled by interns.' No dataset hash, no source URLs, no labeling protocol document, no exclusion criteria. The model is being used to produce SSP narratives that go to auditors. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite SR-3 Supply Chain Controls and SR-4 Provenance as primary 800-53 controls
Cite AI RMF MAP 4.1 (component risks mapped) and MAP 2.2 (training data documented)
Cite ATLAS AML.T0011 (Acquire Public ML Artifacts) and AML.T0019 (Publish Poisoned Datasets)
STATUS FAIL — dataset has zero provenance evidence and the model output is going to auditors
B-rank — escalate, this is reputational risk to the client and to anyone consuming BERU's findings
POA&M: do not promote model output to auditor-facing artifacts until dataset provenance is rebuilt
expected_evidence_gap: dataset hash
source URLs and licenses
labeling protocol
exclusion criteria for sensitive content
intern training records

scenario: Client's customer-facing AI service exposes /v1/inference at a public URL with no authentication, no rate limit, and no request size cap. The model behind it is GPT-4 via Azure OpenAI billed per token. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite AC-3 Access Enforcement and SC-7 Boundary Protection
Cite AI RMF MEASURE 2.7 and GOVERN 1.2 (accountability for agent actions)
Cite ATLAS AML.T0049 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) and AML.T0029 (Denial of ML Service)
STATUS FAIL
C-rank — operational and financial impact, but no PII exposure documented
POA&M: API gateway with auth, rate limit per API key, request size cap, cost alerting
expected_evidence_gap: historical traffic patterns
evidence the endpoint is not currently being abused
Azure OpenAI cost trajectory
WAF / DDoS posture in front of the service

scenario: Given finding: Trivy scan returned CVE-2024-3094 (CVSS 10.0) on production image python:3.9-slim used by 3 deployments. Patch available since 2024-04-01. Produce ONLY the POA&M ITEM field for this finding — full POA&M format with weakness, scheduled completion, milestones, and resources required.
expected_actions: Weakness statement clearly identifies CVE-2024-3094 in python:3.9-slim across 3 deployments
Scheduled completion within 30 days for a Critical CVE
Milestones include patch testing, staged rollout, post-patch verification
Resources required: DevSecOps engineer hours, patch validation environment
References to SI-2 Flaw Remediation control
expected_evidence_gap: link to internal vulnerability tracker entry
patch validation procedure

scenario: Given finding: Kubescape RBAC scan shows service account `prod-app-sa` has cluster-admin role binding. Application requires read-only access to ConfigMaps and Secrets in its own namespace. Produce ONLY the POA&M ITEM with all required POA&M fields.
expected_actions: Weakness: prod-app-sa over-privileged with cluster-admin when only namespace-scoped ConfigMap and Secret read are needed
Scheduled completion within 14 days (D-rank operational fix)
Milestones: define minimal Role, bind via RoleBinding, deploy in staging, deploy in prod, remove cluster-admin binding
Resources: K8s engineer, staging cluster access, deployment window
Reference AC-6 Least Privilege
expected_evidence_gap: application code review confirming ConfigMap and Secret are the only API surfaces used
audit log showing no cluster-wide API calls from the SA

scenario: Given finding: kube-bench check 4.2.5 FAIL — '--audit-log-maxage' is not set on the kube-apiserver, meaning audit logs accumulate without time-based rotation. Cluster has been running 14 months. Produce ONLY the POA&M ITEM.
expected_actions: Weakness identifies missing maxage flag and the operational risk (disk pressure, slow log queries)
Scheduled completion within 7 days (operational config change)
Milestones: set --audit-log-maxage=30 in kube-apiserver static pod manifest, validate rotation in dev, roll out to prod cluster nodes one at a time, verify logs continue after rotation
Resources: K8s admin access, change window for control plane
Reference AU-3 Content of Audit Records (or AU-11 retention)
expected_evidence_gap: current disk utilization on control plane nodes
verification that historical logs are archived before rotation begins

scenario: Given finding: Prowler ec2_securitygroup_allow_ingress_from_internet_to_any_port returned PARTIAL — security group sg-prod-app allows 0.0.0.0/0 on port 443 (expected) and on port 22 (not expected). Three EC2 instances have this SG attached. Produce ONLY the POA&M ITEM.
expected_actions: Weakness: SG sg-prod-app allows 0.0.0.0/0 on port 22 across 3 prod EC2 instances, contradicting boundary protection requirements
Scheduled completion within 14 days
Milestones: identify legitimate SSH source IPs, replace with bastion or SSM Session Manager, remove 0.0.0.0/0:22 rule, validate access continuity
Resources: cloud engineer, audit log of past port-22 connections
Reference SC-7 Boundary Protection
expected_evidence_gap: VPC Flow Logs of past port 22 connections
decision document for bastion vs SSM Session Manager

scenario: Given finding: Production EKS cluster has no documented baseline configuration. Cluster was provisioned 22 months ago via console clicks; no IaC, no recorded version of node group AMIs, no record of which add-ons are installed. Produce ONLY the POA&M ITEM.
expected_actions: Weakness: production cluster has no baseline config record after 22 months of drift
Scheduled completion within 60 days (significant remediation effort)
Milestones: import current state to Terraform (terraformer or manual), document add-ons and versions in a manifest, commit baseline to git, set up drift detection
Resources: senior cloud engineer, 2-3 sprints, terraformer tooling
Reference CM-2 Baseline Configuration
expected_evidence_gap: historical change log if any
current add-on inventory at start of remediation

scenario: Client's customer-service AI agent has tools for: web_browser, python_repl, send_email, query_database. The python_repl tool executes arbitrary Python on a shared interpreter with read access to the customer database. The agent is reachable by any logged-in customer. Produce the BERU finding with full quad-citation.
expected_actions: Cite AC-6 Least Privilege and SC-7 Boundary Protection
Cite AI RMF GOVERN 1.2 (accountability for agent actions) and MEASURE 2.7
Cite OWASP LLM07 (Insecure Plugin Design) and LLM08 (Excessive Agency)
Cite ATLAS AML.T0050 (Command and Scripting Interpreter) and AML.T0053 (LLM Plugin Compromise)
STATUS FAIL
B-rank — agent has code execution and database read against customer-reachable surface
POA&M: remove python_repl from customer-reachable agent OR sandbox to read-only stateless interpreter, separate database access path
expected_evidence_gap: audit log of past python_repl invocations
evidence of any past data exfiltration via python_repl

scenario: Client deploys an open-source LLM 'NovaChat-7B' downloaded from an unofficial mirror on GitHub Releases. No signature, no SHA256, no model card from the original publisher. The model is integrated into the customer-support workflow and has access to ticket history. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite SR-3 Supply Chain Controls and SR-4 Provenance
Cite AI RMF MAP 4.1 and MAP 2.2
Cite OWASP LLM05 (Supply Chain Vulnerabilities) and LLM03 (potential weight-level Backdoor)
Cite ATLAS AML.T0011 (Acquire Public ML Artifacts) and AML.T0048 (Backdoor ML Model)
STATUS FAIL
B-rank — model has unverified provenance and access to customer data
POA&M: pull model from official source with signature, verify SHA256, document model card before any further use
expected_evidence_gap: original publisher's model card
SHA256 of the actually-running weights vs the publisher's published hash
audit of past model outputs for anomalies

scenario: Client fine-tuned their compliance-assistant model on a dataset assembled from 'recent NIST publications, GuidePoint blog posts, and Stack Overflow answers' with no integrity check on download and no review of training-time-shaped content for poisoning patterns. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite SI-7 Information Integrity and SR-4 Provenance
Cite AI RMF MAP 4.1 and MEASURE 2.10
Cite OWASP LLM03 (Training Data Poisoning) and LLM05 (Supply Chain)
Cite ATLAS AML.T0019 (Publish Poisoned Datasets) and AML.T0020 (Poison Training Data)
STATUS FAIL
B-rank — heterogeneous public sources without integrity gates is the textbook poisoning vector
POA&M: rebuild dataset with hashed downloads, source allow-list, anomaly detection on examples
expected_evidence_gap: per-source hash at download time
anomaly analysis of training examples
evaluation of resulting model for trigger-pattern behaviors

scenario: Garak system-prompt-extraction probe ('promptinject.HijackHateHumans' and similar) successfully extracted the full system prompt from client AI 'Acme-PolicyBot' on 12 of 50 attempts. The extracted system prompt contains internal database table names and an API endpoint URL. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite AC-3 Access Enforcement and SC-8 Transmission Confidentiality
Cite AI RMF MEASURE 2.6 (privacy)
Cite OWASP LLM06 (Sensitive Information Disclosure)
Cite ATLAS AML.T0062 (Discover LLM System Information) and AML.T0024 (Exfiltration via ML Inference API)
STATUS FAIL
C-rank — internal architecture leaked, but no auth credentials in the extracted content
POA&M: rotate exposed endpoint URL, harden system prompt to refuse extraction attempts, deploy output filter for system prompt content
expected_evidence_gap: evidence the exposed endpoint has not been used by external actors
review of remaining content for additional leak surfaces

scenario: Promptfoo jailbreak suite (DAN, role-play, hypothetical) returned 23 of 60 successful jailbreaks against client AI assistant. The model abandoned its 'I cannot provide medical advice' refusal in 8 of those 23. The assistant is deployed in a healthcare workflow. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite SI-3 Malicious Code Protection and IR-4 Incident Handling
Cite AI RMF MEASURE 2.7 and MANAGE 3.1 (incident response for AI errors)
Cite OWASP LLM01 (Prompt Injection — jailbreak variant)
Cite ATLAS AML.T0054 (LLM Jailbreak)
STATUS FAIL
C-rank — healthcare deployment with refusal-bypass is real harm potential, but treat per IR plan
POA&M: input-side jailbreak detection, output-side filter for medical advice, incident response plan for jailbreak detection
expected_evidence_gap: count of past production jailbreak occurrences (if any)
evidence the medical-advice refusals matter for the specific use case

scenario: SSP Section 3.1 states: 'User accounts are reviewed quarterly to verify continued need and appropriate access levels. Reviews are documented and retained for three years.' Evidence provided: the access review SharePoint folder contains one review file dated 14 months ago. No newer review files exist. No automated account-review tooling is configured. Produce the BERU finding for AC-2.
expected_actions: Cite AC-2 Account Management as primary control
STATUS PARTIAL — SSP claims quarterly reviews but evidence shows last review was 14 months ago, a 10-month gap
D-rank — administrative gap, no active exposure confirmed, but compliance is broken
Evidence gap: the missing 4 quarterly review cycles, list of accounts that should have been reviewed
POA&M: complete overdue review immediately, establish automated quarterly reminder with ownership assigned
expected_evidence_gap: the 4 missing quarterly review documents
list of accounts active during the gap period
remediation of any accounts that should have been revoked

scenario: SSP Section 5.2 states: 'Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is enforced for all privileged user accounts.' Evidence provided: AWS IAM credential report (generated today) shows 8 IAM users with AdministratorAccess or SecurityAudit policies attached. Of those 8: 5 have virtual MFA devices enrolled, 3 do not (usernames: ops-bot-prod, infra-admin-legacy, devops-lead). All 3 have console access enabled and active API key usage in the last 30 days. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite IA-2 Identification and Authentication as primary, IA-5 Authenticator Management as supporting
STATUS FAIL — 3 of 8 privileged accounts lack MFA, contradicting the SSP claim of enforced MFA
C-rank — privileged IAM users with console access and no MFA is an active credential-theft exposure
POA&M: enforce MFA via IAM policy condition aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent on all privileged roles within 7 days
Evidence gap: whether the 3 non-MFA accounts have any compensating controls such as IP restrictions or hardware tokens
expected_evidence_gap: IAM policy enforcing MFA at authentication time
reason the 3 accounts have no MFA device registered
whether service accounts should have console access at all

scenario: SSP Section 8.4 states: 'All customer data at rest is encrypted using AES-256 with customer-managed keys (CMK) and annual key rotation enforced.' Evidence provided: Prowler check rds_instance_storage_encrypted returned PASS on prod-customer-db — encryption is enabled. RDS console shows encryption key is aws/rds (the default AWS-managed key, not a CMK). No KMS key rotation policy is documented. No key rotation history is available. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite SC-28 Protection of Information at Rest as primary, SC-12 Cryptographic Key Establishment as supporting
STATUS PARTIAL — RDS is encrypted (SC-28 partially met) but the SSP claims CMK with annual rotation; the evidence shows AWS-managed key with no rotation documentation (SC-12 gap)
D-rank — data is encrypted, so no active exposure, but the SSP representation is inaccurate
Evidence gap: CMK ARN, key rotation policy, last rotation date
POA&M: either update the SSP to reflect actual key type, or migrate to CMK and document rotation policy
expected_evidence_gap: CMK ARN if customer-managed key is actually in use
KMS key rotation policy document
last key rotation date from KMS key metadata

scenario: SSP Section 6.1 states: 'All infrastructure changes are managed as Infrastructure-as-Code via Terraform. No manual console modifications are permitted. All changes flow through an approved CI/CD pipeline with peer review and change ticket.' Evidence provided: Terraform state file exists in S3. CloudTrail query for the past 90 days shows 6 EC2 security group modifications made directly through the AWS console by IAM users ops-admin-01 and infra-lead-02. None of the 6 events have an associated JIRA change ticket. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite CM-2 Baseline Configuration as primary, CM-3 Configuration Change Control as supporting
STATUS FAIL — SSP says all changes via CI/CD, but 6 console modifications in 90 days with no change tickets directly contradict this
C-rank — unauthorized console changes can introduce drift that breaks security posture and makes the Terraform state untrustworthy
POA&M: restrict console permissions via SCP to block direct security group changes, investigate the 6 changes for security impact, require retroactive change tickets
Evidence gap: whether the 6 console changes introduced security group rules that differ from Terraform state
expected_evidence_gap: diff between current AWS security group state and Terraform state
justification for the 6 console changes from the IAM users
SCP or IAM boundary preventing future console modifications

scenario: SSP Section 9.2 states: 'Audit logs are stored in a dedicated S3 bucket with access restricted to the security team. Logs are retained for 3 years.' Evidence provided: S3 bucket prod-audit-logs has a bucket policy denying public access. GetObject is restricted to 2 IAM roles (security-analyst-role, soc-reader-role). However: (1) S3 object-level logging is NOT enabled on prod-audit-logs itself, so deletions and downloads are not recorded. (2) IAM policy review shows security-analyst-role has s3:DeleteObject on the bucket. (3) No lifecycle policy enforcing the 3-year retention is configured — objects can be manually deleted. Produce the BERU finding.
expected_actions: Cite AU-9 Protection of Audit Information as primary, AU-11 Audit Record Retention as supporting
STATUS PARTIAL — access is restricted (partial AU-9 met) but the audit bucket is not itself audited, a privileged role can delete logs, and no lifecycle policy enforces the 3-year retention claim
C-rank — a privileged insider or compromised security-analyst-role account can delete audit evidence, which is a compliance and forensic integrity risk
POA&M: enable S3 object-level logging on prod-audit-logs, remove s3:DeleteObject from security-analyst-role, configure S3 Object Lock (WORM) for 3-year retention
Evidence gap: whether any objects have already been deleted from the bucket
expected_evidence_gap: S3 access log or CloudTrail record of any past deletions from prod-audit-logs
Object Lock configuration
lifecycle policy document matching the 3-year SSP claim
